content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 21, 2007

Boyd L. Hill
McCormick, Kidman & Behrens, LLP
650 Town Center Dr., Ste 100
Costa Mesa, California 92626-7187

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-013

Dear Mr. Hill:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Jim Snyder, a board member of the San Gorgonio Pass Water Agency, regarding the conflicts of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. May a board member of the San Gorgonio Pass Water Agency ("Agency") vote on a proposed agreement under which the Agency would assist the City of Banning Heights ("City") and the Banning Heights Mutual Water Company ("Company") (collectively "Buyers") with the purchase of water transport facilities (the "Facilities") from Southern California Edison ("SCE")? The Buyers provide water service to properties owned by the board member located several miles from the Facilities.

2. May the board member serve on a committee of the Agency tasked with reviewing the proposed purchase agreement?

CONCLUSION

1 and 2. Yes. Under the facts presented, the board member may participate and vote on board matters, including the committee, pertaining to the proposed agreement so long as the specific circumstances listed in regulation 18705.2(b) are not present to rebut the presumption of no material effect, as discussed below.

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Agency is a State Water Project contractor with the obligation to provide
water from the State Water Project to the San Gorgonio Pass area. Jim Snyder, a member
of the Agency's board, has authorized you to request advice on his behalf regarding a
decision of the Agency to enter into an agreement ("Agreement") with SCE and the
Buyers under which the Buyers would purchase from SCE its facilities that transport
water from the Whitewater River to the San Gorgonio River bed ("Facilities"). The
Buyers already own the rights to this water and operate wells at the San Gorgonio River.
Transfer of the Facilities will not change the source, amount or quality of the
water that
Buyers supply to customers. It may result in increased certainty of the existing supply.
As part of the Facilities transfer, SCE will be required to make some repairs to the
Facilities. The proposed agreement contemplates the Agency providing assistance with a
portion of the Buyers' costs and in obtaining necessary permits. The Agency is forming a
committee for the purpose of reviewing the Agreement. The board member would like to
serve on this committee.

The board member's residence is located on one of five lots he owns in the
unincorporated area of Banning Bend. The Company, a nonprofit mutual water company
in which property owners are not only water purchasers but are shareholders of the
Company, provides water to properties in Banning Bend. Only three of the five lots have
water service connections. The Riverside County Health Department has prohibited
additional connections until a second source of water is developed. The contemplated
transfer of the Facilities does not provide an additional source of water. The board
member also owns residential property in the City which he rents to his granddaughter at
the cost of his mortgage and insurance payments. In our conversation of February 9,
2007, you advised that this property has never been rented to anyone else and that it was
specifically purchased for the granddaughter's use. Water for this property is purchased
from the City. The board member's properties are located several miles from the
Facilities.

ANALYSIS

Your question implicates the Act's conflict of interest provisions which ensure
that public officials "perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported
them." (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
(Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 3

analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a
given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

1. & 2. Is the board member a "public official" and will he be making,
or influencing a governmental decision?

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act pertain only to public officials. A
public official includes "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
governmental agency ...." (Section 82048; regulation 18701(a)(1).) As a board member
of the Agency, he is a public official subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702.1.) The board member
will "make a governmental decision" if he votes on the Agreement that would obligate
the Agency to pay for some of the Buyer's costs of purchasing the Facilities.

If the board member engages in negotiations without significant substantive
review, provides advice or makes recommendations, he will be "participating" in the
decision (regulation 18702.2). He will be "influencing" that decision if he appears before
or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
Agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) Thus, by serving on the committee that will review the
Agreement, the board member would be participating in making and influencing a
governmental decision.

3. What are the board member's economic interests — the possible sources
of a conflict of interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:

• an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

• an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 4

• an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

• an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); regulation
18703.4.)

In addition, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)

Economic Interests Disclosed in your Request for Advice

Rental Business:

The property the board member rents out may be considered to be a real estate
business owned by him. If the board member has an investment of $2,000 or more in this
business, he has an economic interest in the business. (Section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a).) However, section 82005 provides that "business entity" means any
organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or
association. (Emphasis added.) Under the particular circumstances you have described
in which the property was purchased to provide for his granddaughter and she pays the
equivalent of the mortgage and insurance payments, we conclude that he is not operating
the rental property for profit.

Ownership Interest in the Company:

You indicate that the Company is a non-profit organization. Accordingly, the
board member's interest in the Company's shares is not an economic interest in a
business entity because, as stated above, section 82005 defines "business entity" so as to
only include entities operated for profit. (Christensen Advice Letter No. A-97-536.)

Real Property:

The board member owns six properties in which he has an interest of $2,000 or
more. These include his residence, the four unimproved lots in Banning Bench and the
residential property occupied by his granddaughter in the City.

Source of Income:

Because the board member receives rent from his granddaughter, she is
considered to be a source of income to him if she pays him $500 or more within 12

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 5

months prior to his making a governmental decision. Presumably, she pays him $500 or
more per year. "Income" is defined in section 82030 as including "rent" without any
allowance for offsetting expenses.

Personal Finances:

The board member also has an economic interest in his personal finances and
those of his immediate family. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.) A governmental
decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in the
personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of an official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing. (Ibid.) Your letter does not mention any impact on the
board member's personal finances as the result of the upcoming decisions; therefore we
do not further analyze this issue. However, please note that a conflict of interest may
arise if it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the Agreement will have an
effect on his personal finances.

4. Are the board member's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?

Real Property:

Regulation 18704.2 sets forth the circumstances under which real property is
considered directly involved in a governmental decision:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real
property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries)
of the property which is the subject of the governmental decision. For purposes
of subdivision (a)(5), real property is located 'within 500 feet of the boundaries
(or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
governmental decision' if any part of the real property is within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.

"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation or
deannexation, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city,
county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property.
For purposes of this subdivision, the terms 'zoning' and 'rezoning' shall refer to
the act of establishing or changing the zoning or land use designation on the real
property in which the official has an interest.

"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of
the real property in which the official has an interest.

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 6

"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of
any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official
has an interest.

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select the
project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to
certify the environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it
is located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment area.

"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to streets, water,
sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the
official has an interest will receive new or improved services."

The decision to enter into an agreement to spend Agency funds to assist with the
cost of transferring the Facilities does not fall within any of the circumstances listed
above. The Facilities are located on land that is well beyond 500 feet of any of the board
member's properties. The decision does not involve zoning, annexation, sale, purchase,
lease, exchange, transfer or subdivision of the board member's real property. The
decision does not involve taxes or fees affecting his real property, nor does it involve
survey areas, project areas, preliminary plans, environmental documents, or
redevelopment plans or areas. Finally, inasmuch as the Agreement merely involves the
transfer of the Facilities, obligates SCE only to make certain repairs in connection with
the transfer, and will not result in any change to the source, amount or quality of water
supplies, the decision to enter into the Agreement does not involve "construction of, or
improvements to streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities." The board
member's real property is, therefore, indirectly involved in the decision.²

Source of Income:

Regulation 18704.1 provides that "a person, including business entities, sources
of income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's

² You raise the issue that even if the board member's real property were to be deemed directly
involved in the governmental decision under the above criteria, the exception of subdivision (b)(2) would
apply because SCE's responsibility under the Agreement is limited to repairs and not improvements.
Subdivision (b)(2) provides that notwithstanding a public official's economic interest being directly
involved in a governmental decision, it is deemed indirectly involved if the decision solely concerns
repairs, replacement or maintenance of existing streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities.
We need not rely on this exception because we have determined that under regulation 18704.2(a) the board
member's real property is indirectly involved. The board member should, however, be alert to future board
decisions which may involve construction of, or improvements to, the Facilities, in which case his property
would be directly involved under regulation 18704.2(a)(6).

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 7

agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request; or (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency." (Emphasis added.) Since the board member's granddaughter, a source of income to him, is not initiating proceedings regarding the Facilities or the Agreement nor is she a party in or the subject of any such proceeding, this economic interest is deemed to be indirectly involved in the governmental decision.

5. Applicable Materiality Standard.

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the agency's decision.

Real Property:

Regulation 18705.2(b) sets forth the applicable materiality standard for the board member's real property interests. It provides that the "financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption maybe rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the public has an economic interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest." Examples of these specific circumstances include circumstances where the decision affects:

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;

"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;

"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

Inasmuch as the proposed Agreement will not change the source, amount or quality of water available to the board member's properties, it does not appear the

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 8

presumption is rebutted. However, we are not in a position to make decisions as to
whether there is enough "proof" of specific circumstances to rebut the presumption.

Source of Income:

Regulation 18705.3 provides the materiality standard for an economic interest in
persons who are sources of income to the board member. For indirectly involved sources
of income, the financial effect of a governmental decision is deemed material with
respect to an individual who is a source of income to an official if any of the following
applies:

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $ 1,000 or more; or

"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18705.2(b)." (Regulation 18705.3(b)(3).)

The only source of income you identified is the board member's granddaughter. While
she may have a real property interest in the house she occupies, pursuant to subdivision
(B) and regulation 18705.2(b), it would only be considered indirectly involved in the
decision. As stated above, under regulation 18705.2(b), "the financial effect of a
governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed not to be material." The circumstances under which the
presumption may be rebutted are listed in subsection (b)(1) for interests in real property
other than leaseholds and in subsection (b)(2) for leasehold interests. None appear
applicable to the facts you have provided and therefore the presumption would appear to
stand.

6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon the board member's economic interests will meet the
applicable materiality standard? (Does a conflict of interest result?)

A material financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to that
economic interest will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable,
but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 9

Real Property:

The question of whether financial consequences are reasonably foreseeable at the
time a governmental decision is made must always depend on the facts of each particular
case. (In re Thorner, supra.) You have not advised us of facts that would tend to rebut
the presumption that the governmental decision will not have a material financial effect
on the board member's real property. You state that the transfer of the Facilities "may
result in greater certainty" for existing water supplies but "will not increase the amount or
quality of water available" to property owners. These facts would appear to support a
conclusion that the presumption should stand.

Source of Income:

It is not clear from your letter whether the board member's granddaughter rents
the property month-to-month or if she has a leasehold interest in the property.³ If she is
deemed to have a leasehold interest, then under regulation 18705.2(b), the financial effect
of the governmental decision is presumed not to be material. If not, we reach a similar
result because it does not appear that the standard under regulation 18705.3 is met.

The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) ) FPC Ops. 71.) The determination of whether or not it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for the board member's
economic interests is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately for him to decide.

7. The "Public Generally" Exception.

Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be
able to take a role in the governmental decision in question. If the reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect of a governmental decision on the public official's economic
interest is indistinguishable "from its effect on the public generally," then the public
official does not have a conflict. (Section 87103; regulations 18700(b)(7), 18707(a).)
Under the facts you have provided, it does not appear that the "public generally"
exception applies.⁴

___________________________

³ Regulation 18233 provides that the terms "interest in real property" and "leasehold interest" as
used in Government Code Section 82033 shall not include the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy of
one month or less.

⁴ You indicate the board member owns multiple properties. The ownership of multiple interests
does not automatically preclude application of the "public generally" exception. However, under your
facts, it would appear that ownership of six residential lots, four of which are one acre, one of which is five
acres, and two of which are occupied by small residences, would result in a financial effect on the board
member that is not "substantially the same" as the effect on a significant segment of property owners or
homeowners.

File No. A-07-013
Page No. 10


8. Legal Required Participation.

You have not provided any information regarding the application of the final step, step eight regarding the exception for "legal required participation."

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

Regulation 18704.2 sets forth the circumstances under which real property is

of the property wfrich is the subject of the govemmentA aecision. for prrpor",
of subdivision (a)(5), real property is locatid 'within 500 feet of the boundaries
(or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
govemmental decision' if any partof the real property is within 500 feet of the

(*city") and the Banning Heights Mutual water dompany (.,comp any,)(collectively

california Edison ("scE')? The Buyers provide water service to properties owned by
the board member located several miles from the Facilities.
2' May the board member serye on a committee of the Agency tasked with
reviewing the proposed purchase agreement?

CONCLUSION: I and 2' Yes. Under the facts presented, the board member may participate and
vote on board matters, including the committee, pertaining to trr" propor.J agreement so
long as the specific circumstances listed in regutatio n 18,7"05.21b) are not pr"J*t to rebut

The Agency is a State Water Project contractor with the obligation to provide

Facilities. The proposed agreement contemplat"r th" Agency providing assistance with a

The board member's residence is located on one of five lots he owns in the

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 February 21, 2007 Boyd L. Hill McCormick, Kidman & Behrens, LLP 650 Town Center Dr., Ste 100 Costa Mesa, California 92626-7187 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-07-013 Dear Mr. Hill: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Jim Snyder, a board member of the San Gorgonio Pass Water Agency, regarding the conflicts of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. May a board member of the San Gorgonio Pass Water Agency ("Agency") vote on a proposed agreement under which the Agency would assist the City of Banning Heights ("City") and the Banning Heights Mutual Water Company ("Company") (collectively "Buyers") with the purchase of water transport facilities (the "Facilities") from Southern California Edison ("SCE")? The Buyers provide water service to properties owned by the board member located several miles from the Facilities. 2. May the board member serve on a committee of the Agency tasked with reviewing the proposed purchase agreement? CONCLUSION 1 and 2. Yes. Under the facts presented, the board member may participate and vote on board matters, including the committee, pertaining to the proposed agreement so long as the specific circumstances listed in regulation 18705.2(b) are not present to rebut the presumption of no material effect, as discussed below. ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-07-013 Page No. 2 FACTS The Agency is a State Water Project contractor with the obligation to provide water from the State Water Project to the San Gorgonio Pass area. Jim Snyder, a member of the Agency's board, has authorized you to request advice on his behalf regarding a decision of the Agency to enter into an agreement ("Agreement") with SCE and the Buyers under which the Buyers would purchase from SCE its facilities that transport water from the Whitewater River to the San Gorgonio River bed ("Facilities"). The Buyers already own the rights to this water and operate wells at the San Gorgonio River. Transfer of the Facilities will not change the source, amount or quality of the water that Buyers supply to customers. It may result in increased certainty of the existing supply. As part of the Facilities transfer, SCE will be required to make some repairs to the Facilities. The proposed agreement contemplates the Agency providing assistance with a portion of the Buyers' costs and in obtaining necessary permits. The Agency is forming a committee for the purpose of reviewing the Agreement. The board member would like to serve on this committee. The board member's residence is located on one of five lots he owns in the unincorporated area of Banning Bend. The Company, a nonprofit mutual water company in which property owners are not only water purchasers but are shareholders of the Company, provides water to properties in Banning Bend. Only
